title: Chapter 19: The Letter in the Wall
content: The morning tastes like coffee I didn't drink.

Rowan left his espresso on the nightstand — full, cold, a skin of crema gone grey — and I can still taste the ghost of it on my teeth, bitter and mineral, because I kissed him before we left and his mouth was stained with it. The hotel room smelled like stone dust from the palazzo and the green-soap scent of his skin. Now we're walking back through Dorsoduro, the canal light cutting silver across the calle, and I'm thinking about how he looked at breakfast: focused, meticulous, the way he looks at contracts. The palazzo is a contract. He wants to inspect the progress.
I let him lead.

The scaffolding went up last week. Green netting, iron poles, the plywood walkways that turn a palazzo into a construction site. The workers arrive at seven. We arrive at eight, and the place is already loud — the whine of saws, the percussion of hammer on nail, the grinding drone of something being stripped. The air inside is thick with brick dust. It coats my tongue. It settles into the creases of my hands. I rub my fingers together and feel the grit, fine as ash, and I think: this is what three centuries reduced to. Dust and骨架.

The palazzo has been stripped to its skeleton. The wallpaper — that late-nineteenth-century damask I always hated — is gone. The plaster beneath it is gouged and scored, patchy as mange, revealing the brickwork underneath. Red brick, the color of dried blood. The color of Venetian earth. Some of the bricks are original; you can tell by the softness of their edges, the way they've been worn by time and salt air into something almost organic. The beams overhead are ancient — dark oak, hand-hewn, blackened with age. The ceiling they once held up is partly gone, and through the gaps I can see the floor above, and above that, a sliver of Venetian sky, white as bone.

Rowan is talking to one of the construction workers. I don't catch the man's name. He's wearing a hard hat and a fluorescent vest, and his hands move when he talks — pointing to the beams, the walls, the places where the plaster has been removed to check the structural integrity. Rowan nods. He listens carefully. He always listens carefully. It's one of the things I've loved across every life: the way he gives his full attention to whatever is in front of him, as if the world contracts to a single point and that point is all that matters.
I'm not listening. I'm looking at the wall.

The wall on the east side of the main salon. The wall where the fireplace used to be — they removed the mantelpiece yesterday, apparently, because there's a raw patch of plaster and exposed brick where it sat for two hundred years. The brick is beautiful in its nakedness. Imperfect. Some of the mortar has crumbled away, leaving gaps between the bricks like missing teeth, and the cold Venetian air pushes through those gaps, and I can feel it on my face — damp, mineral, carrying the green smell of the canal.
Behind the construction worker's shoulder, I see it.

A gap between bricks, wider than the others. Something wedged inside. Not mortar. Not debris. Something deliberate. The edges are pale and fibrous. Parchment. Folded. And on the fold — a disc of red wax, chipped but intact, pressed flat against the fragment of plaster that still clings to the brick.
My heart stops.

Not metaphorically. It stops. I forget to breathe. The construction worker is still talking — something about load-bearing walls and the timeline for the second floor — and his voice becomes a murmur, white noise, the sound of water over stone. All I can hear is the blood rushing in my ears and the crinkle of parchment, imagined, a sound that hasn't happened yet but that I can hear as clearly as if someone were unfolding it right now.

I know that seal. I pressed that seal. I heated the wax over a candle — tallow, it would have been tallow, beeswax was for the Church — and I pressed my thumb into it because I didn't have a signet ring, and the whorls of my fingerprint are still visible in the red surface if you look closely enough. Three hundred years, and my fingerprint is still there.

I wrote it in 1743. The letter to Marco. The letter I wrote the night after I bricked the wall closed, my hands raw with lime and sand, my fingernails cracked. I wrote it because I couldn't bear to leave nothing. I wrote it because I needed the words to exist somewhere, even if no one ever read them, even if the wall held them longer than any human life. I bricked the letter into the wall so it would survive him. So it would survive all of them.
It survived.

It's right there. Behind the plaster that the renovation has stripped away. Three centuries of plaster — layer after layer, damask on limewash on gesso on plaster — and now it's exposed. After all this time. After every careful, deliberate year of hiding what I am, the wall has opened like a mouth and the secret is sitting there, folded and sealed, waiting to be heard.
I reach for it without thinking.
My hand is in the air, my fingers reaching toward the gap in the brick, and the construction worker turns. He sees my hand. He sees where I'm reaching. And he says, "Oh — we found that yesterday."
I freeze.

He's casual about it. He doesn't know what he's holding in his gaze, what he's standing in front of. He's a man who works with old buildings; he finds things. "Looks like some old love letter," he says, and he reaches past me and slides it out of the gap with the careless ease of someone extracting a splinter. The parchment makes a sound — a soft, dry crinkle, like the whisper of a page turning in an empty library — and the wax seal catches the light from the gap in the ceiling above. Red. Still red. The iron in the pigment has held.

"The handwriting's incredible," the worker says, holding it loosely, not reverently, because to him it's a curiosity, not a confession. "You can barely tell it's that old. Usually the ink fades, the paper goes to mush. But this — it's like someone wrote it last week."
It's like someone wrote it last week.

I wrote it last week, if last week were measured in decades. If time were a room I walked through instead of a river I drowned in. The handwriting is the same because the hand is the same. Because I am the same Orlando who sat at a table in this very palazzo, in a room that doesn't exist anymore, and pressed an iron gall pen into rag paper and wrote the words I couldn't say out loud. The words I bricked into the wall because I couldn't trust the living to carry them.
Rowan's hand closes over the parchment before mine does.

He takes it from the construction worker with a nod and a thanks and a small smile — that effortless charm, that social grace that makes people forget he's just taken something — and then he unfolds it. The parchment resists. It's brittle at the creases. He's careful with it; he's always careful with old things. The wax seal cracks along its center — I hear it, a sound like a tiny bone breaking — but the letter opens.
He reads it.

I watch his eyes move. Left to right, left to right. The construction worker has retreated to check something on the far side of the room, and we're alone in the stripped salon with the scaffolding overhead and the cold air coming through the gaps in the brick, and Rowan is reading the letter I wrote three hundred years ago to the man he used to be.
He reads aloud. Not all of it. Just the first line, because that's all it takes.
"My beloved, I have loved you in masks and in moonlight—"
His voice catches.

The sound he makes isn't a gasp. It's smaller than that. It's the sound of a man stepping on ice he didn't know was there. The kind of sound you make when the ground shifts beneath you and for a half second you're not sure it's going to hold.
He looks up at me.

And what I see in his face isn't confusion. It isn't suspicion. It isn't the polite bewilderment of someone encountering an anomaly. It's wakefulness. Pure, cracking, terrible wakefulness. Like a man who has been sleepwalking through something — through months of conversations, through a woman he can't quite figure out, through a pull he's been explaining away with chemistry and coincidence — and has just hit a wall.

The wall. This wall. The wall that has been holding my words since before he was born, before his grandfather was born, before the Republic fell and the Austrians came and left and the world remade itself a dozen times over.
He holds the letter in both hands. The parchment trembles — or his hands do.
"This is your handwriting," he says.
Not a question.
"Yes."
The word comes out of me like a stone. Heavy and final and exactly where I've been trying not to put it.
"It says 1743."
"Yes."

He looks at the letter again. At the date, inked in the upper right corner, in the same hand that signed my lease last month. The same hand that held his coffee cup this morning. The same hand that bricked this letter into a wall when George II was king and Venice was a republic gasping its last.
"You wrote a love letter in 1743 and bricked it into the wall of this palazzo, and you're standing here in 2026, and the handwriting is the same."
"I understand how that sounds."

I don't know why I say it. It's the most inadequate thing I've ever said. It sounds like a lie offered to a man who has just seen the truth. It sounds like the kind of thing you say when you're trying to manage someone's reaction, and that's exactly what I'm doing — still, even now, even with the evidence in his hands — trying to manage it. Trying to make it bearable.
"Do you?" he says, and his voice is steady, but his hands are not. He's trembling. The parchment flutters. "Because it sounds impossible."

He's not angry. I've seen him angry. Angry Rowan is cold and precise and surgical; he cuts with words, dissects with questions, leaves nothing bleeding. This isn't that. He's not dismissive, either. Dismissive Rowan shrugs and changes the subject and files the inexplicable under "not relevant to current operations." He's not doing that either.

He's trembling with the effort of containing something too large for the framework of his understanding. I know that effort. I lived inside it for centuries before I learned that some truths don't fit into frames — they burn the frame down and stand in the ashes and dare you to look away. Rowan is standing in the ashes now, and he is not looking away.

The construction worker calls something from across the room — a question about timelines, about whether they should proceed with the second-floor demolition. Rowan doesn't answer. He doesn't take his eyes off me. The man shrugs and returns to his work, and the saw starts up again, and the dust keeps falling — fine red brick dust that settles on our shoulders like snow, like ash, like the residue of everything that has been destroyed to get us to this moment.

I can smell it. The dust, yes — dry and mineral and faintly metallic, like blood without the copper. But under that, the cold Venetian air, canal-water and stone and the green rot of something organic decaying in the laguna. And under that, something older. The ghost of tallow candles and iron gall ink and the lime-sand mortar I mixed with my own hands. The smell of 1743, preserved in the pores of these bricks, released now that the plaster is gone.
Rowan folds the letter.

He does it slowly. Carefully. Along the original creases, which are still visible where the fibers have weakened but haven't broken. He aligns the edges with the precision of a man who respects paper, who has spent his career understanding that documents are alive — that they breathe, that they carry weight, that they can shift the world. He folds it once, twice, and then he slides it into the inside pocket of his jacket, against his chest, over his heart.
He looks at me.

The scaffolding throws shadows across his face — bars of light and dark, like a cage, like a gate. His eyes are the color of the canal, grey-green, changeable, and right now they are still. Very still. Like water that has found its level and is waiting for whatever comes next.
"I need you to tell me the truth," he says. "All of it."

Not: *Explain this.* Not: *What's going on?* Not the questions of a man grasping for a lifeline. A demand. Steady and certain. The most courageous thing he's ever said, because Rowan — who has built his entire life on controlling information, on knowing more than anyone in the room, on the precision of what he chooses to reveal — is asking for something he cannot control. He knows he can't control it. He's asking anyway.

The brick dust is on my lips. I taste it when I open my mouth — grit and mineral and the faint bitterness of something that was once whole and has been ground down. The coffee I didn't drink. The cold air I've been breathing. The salt of my own skin.

I take a breath that feels like it might be my last as this person. The person who hid. The person who lied by omission, who carried three centuries like a stone in her pocket and pretended the weight was nothing. The person who fell in love with a man for the fourth time and told herself that this time, maybe, she wouldn't have to watch him die without knowing the truth.
That person is done.
"I've lived a very long time," I say. "And you have been in most of it."

The saw stops. Or maybe it doesn't. Maybe I stop hearing it. The dust keeps falling. The letter is in his jacket, over his heart. The wall is open, and the gap where the parchment lived is empty — a small dark space between two bricks, shaped exactly like the thing it held for three hundred years.
He doesn't move. He doesn't speak. He stands in the stripped palazzo with the skeleton of the building around him and the evidence against his chest and my words in the air, and he waits.
So do I.
